question_title: Will at least one No. 1 seed reach the 2026 NCAA Division I men’s basketball national championship game?
summary: The question asks whether at least one No. 1 seed will reach the 2026 NCAA men's basketball championship game. The historical base rate since 1985 is very strong: 31 out of 40 tournaments (77.5%) had at least one #1 seed in the championship game. The recent trend is even more favorable at 90% in the last 10 tournaments. The 2026 projected #1 seeds (Duke, Michigan, Arizona, and Florida/UConn) are all elite programs. There's no specific reason to deviate from the historical norm, so I anchor near the base rate with a slight upward adjustment for recent trends.

factors.description: Strong historical base rate: 31/40 championship games (1985-2025) had at least one #1 seed (77.5%)
Recent trend even stronger: 9/10 of last 10 tournaments (2015-2025) had at least one #1 seed in the title game (90%)
2026 projected #1 seeds are elite programs (Duke, Michigan, Arizona, Florida/UConn) with strong resumes
March Madness upset potential remains - in 9 of 40 tournaments no #1 seed reached the title game (years like 2023, 2014, 2011)

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.79 (79%)**

Key reasoning:
- **Historical base rate (77.5%)**: Since the 64-team era began in 1985, at least one #1 seed has reached the championship game in 31 of 40 tournaments. Only 9 years lacked a #1 seed in the title game (1989, 1991, 1998, 2003, 2004, 2006, 2011, 2014, 2023).
- **Recent trend is stronger (90%)**: In the last 10 tournaments, 9 of 10 had at least one #1 seed finalist. The 2025 tournament was historically chalk — all four #1 seeds reached the Final Four.
- **Strong 2026 projected #1 seeds**: Duke, Michigan, Arizona, and Florida/UConn are all elite programs.
- **Downside risk**: March Madness upsets can eliminate all top seeds (e.g., 2023 featured UConn #4 vs SDSU #5; 2014 featured UConn #7 vs Kentucky #8), but this happens only ~22.5% of the time historically.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if either finalist shown on the official NCAA men’s tournament bracket page at https://www.ncaa.com/news/basketball-men/mml-official-bracket/2026-01-28/2026-ncaa-tournament-printable-bracket-schedule-march-madness was a No. 1 seed in the official bracket. Otherwise, it resolves to no.
fine_print: Use the original official bracket seed lines. Later vacated results do not affect resolution unless the NCAA officially revises the bracket before the deadline. Candidate Sources: NCAA tournament bracket page and related NCAA results pages.
